name: Ray Tune (Distributed Hyperparameter Optimization)

auth.notes: Local Ray cluster; Anyscale for managed cloud

performance.notes: Distributed across Ray cluster; early stopping via ASHA/PBT; scales to 1000s of trials

agent_readiness.notes: Distributed HPO framework built on Ray. from ray import tune. from ray.tune.schedulers import ASHAScheduler. def train_fn(config): model = build_model(config['lr']). tuner = tune.Tuner(train_fn, param_space={'lr': tune.loguniform(1e-4, 1e-1)}). results = tuner.fit(). Schedulers: ASHA (early stopping), Population Based Training (PBT), HyperBand. Search algorithms: Optuna, Bayesian, HyperOpt integration. Multi-GPU, multi-node. PyTorch, TensorFlow, Keras, XGBoost, LightGBM integrations. MLflow/WandB logging. Fault tolerance via checkpointing. Most scalable HPO solution in Python.

gotchas: Ray cluster setup adds significant operational complexity
Debugging distributed trials requires understanding Ray architecture
Overhead is high for small-scale HPO (use Optuna locally instead)
Results object API changed significantly between Ray 1.x and 2.x
best_when: Large-scale distributed HPO across many machines/GPUs
Population Based Training (PBT) for RL and language model training
Teams already using Ray for distributed computing
When Optuna's local execution isn't fast enough
avoid_when: Quick experiments where cluster overhead isn't justified
summary: Ray Tune is the distributed HPO framework built on Ray. tune.Tuner(train_fn, param_space={...}).fit(). Schedulers: ASHA (early stopping), Population Based Training. Search: Optuna/HyperOpt integration. Multi-GPU, multi-node. PyTorch/TF/XGBoost/LightGBM integrations. MLflow/WandB logging. Fault-tolerant via checkpointing. Most scalable Python HPO but heavy overhead — use Optuna for single-machine. Best when already using Ray ecosystem.
